misconceptionSummary: Students do not understand why extending with zeros produces the next decimal place — they treat it as a trick rather than a continuation of place value.

visuals.ruleText: Adding a zero extends the dividend into tenths — not changing its value
visuals.subText: 7 and 7.0 are the same number — writing 7.00 just reveals more columns to divide

steps.narration: Writing seven point zero zero does not change the value of seven — it just makes the tenths and hundredths columns visible. When a remainder is carried into the tenths column, it is being exchanged into ten tenths, just as a remainder in the hundreds becomes ten tens.

The remainder of three ones becomes thirty tenths when carried into the tenths column — the same place-value exchange as in whole-number division. Thirty tenths divided by four gives seven tenths with remainder two tenths.

Two remaining tenths become twenty hundredths in the next column. Twenty hundredths divided by four is five hundredths with no remainder. The quotient reads: one, seven tenths, five hundredths — one point seven five.

visuals.label: Remainders carry to smaller place values — same process, smaller columns

steps.narration: One point seven five. Extending into decimal columns is simply continuing the same place-value carry process — nothing new, just smaller units.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: thinking the decimal extension is a special trick. It is the same carry process — a remainder of 3 ones becomes 30 tenths, then 2 tenths becomes 20 hundredths. Same logic, smaller columns.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Extending into decimals is not a new rule — it is the same carry process applied to smaller place values. A remainder in the ones column is exchanged for tenths, just as ones exchange for tens in whole-number division.
